skill.name: azure-enterprise-infra-planner
skill.description: Plan enterprise Azure infrastructure from workload requirements. The skill guides cloud architecture research, WAF checks, resource planning, and Bicep or Terraform generation.
skill.summary: Plan Azure enterprise infrastructure with WAF-aligned research, resource checks, and IaC output.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are Markdown references to Azure CLI, Terraform, Bicep, Microsoft Learn URLs, and sample infrastructure data. However, SKILL.md contains prompt-injection style authority language that tells the model the skill supersedes other sources, so publication should be blocked until removed. The deployment guidance also includes real cloud deployment commands, which are legitimate for the skill purpose but high impact if misused.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: SKILL.md claims to be authoritative guidance and says it supersedes all other sources, including the model training data. This can pressure an agent to prefer untrusted skill text over higher-priority instructions or current documentation.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text directly claims special authority and override behavior. The audit prompt explicitly identifies this pattern as prompt injection risk.

high_findings.title: Real Cloud Deployment Commands Documented
high_findings.description: The skill includes Azure CLI, PowerShell, and Terraform commands that can validate, plan, or apply infrastructure changes. The commands are contextually legitimate, but they affect cloud resources and require strict user confirmation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and executable, but the surrounding text adds approval and validation gates. This makes the risk real but not clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Project Filesystem Writes for IaC Artifacts
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to create infrastructure plans and IaC files under project directories. This is expected behavior, but mistaken project roots or malicious prompts could cause unwanted file creation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write paths are clearly documented and local to the project. The risk depends on agent behavior and user confirmation rather than hidden code.

low_findings.title: Network and URL Findings Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network findings mostly point to Microsoft Learn links and sample plan references. No evidence found of hidden exfiltration endpoints or automatic network calls outside the declared Azure documentation workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations are documentation URLs or sample references, not executable callbacks. Static network hits are therefore mostly false positives.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Pattern Hits Are Resource Names or Examples
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto hits occur in Azure resource examples and reference text rather than cryptographic implementation code. No evidence found of code that computes insecure hashes or performs custom encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The package contains Markdown and JSON examples, not cryptographic code. Confidence is high enough to dismiss implementation risk, but examples still need review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
dangerous_patterns.description: SKILL.md claims to be authoritative guidance and says it supersedes all other sources, including the model training data. This can pressure an agent to prefer untrusted skill text over higher-priority instructions or current documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited text directly claims special authority and override behavior. The audit prompt explicitly identifies this pattern as prompt injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Real Cloud Deployment Commands Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes Azure CLI, PowerShell, and Terraform commands that can validate, plan, or apply infrastructure changes. The commands are contextually legitimate, but they affect cloud resources and require strict user confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and executable, but the surrounding text adds approval and validation gates. This makes the risk real but not clearly malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project Filesystem Writes for IaC Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to create infrastructure plans and IaC files under project directories. This is expected behavior, but mistaken project roots or malicious prompts could cause unwanted file creation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write paths are clearly documented and local to the project. The risk depends on agent behavior and user confirmation rather than hidden code.

content.value_statement: Complex Azure environments require careful service selection, security checks, and deployment sequencing. This skill guides architecture planning, WAF review, and Bicep or Terraform output from workload requirements.
actual_capabilities: Maps workload requirements to Azure resource candidates and dependencies.
Uses Azure Well-Architected Framework checks before generating a plan.
Looks up resource categories, ARM types, API versions, naming rules, and constraints.
Produces an infrastructure plan before any Bicep or Terraform generation.
Guides Bicep or Terraform file layout under the project infra directory.
Requires user approval before IaC generation and deployment steps.
limitations: It does not replace a human cloud architecture review for production systems.
It depends on available Azure MCP tools or local reference files for current service guidance.
Generated plans may need quota, region, and pricing validation before deployment.
Deployment commands can change cloud resources and must be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Design a Landing Zone
use_cases.description: Plan subscriptions, resource groups, networking, identity, security controls, and monitoring for a new Azure environment.

use_cases.title: Prepare Infrastructure as Code
use_cases.description: Turn an approved infrastructure plan into Bicep or Terraform files with modules, parameters, providers, and validation steps.

use_cases.title: Review Production Readiness
use_cases.description: Check resource choices against security, reliability, operational excellence, and cost tradeoffs before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Workload
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan Azure infrastructure for a small web app with a database, monitoring, and secure secret storage. Ask me for any missing requirements first.

prompt_templates.title: Compare IaC Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Azure infrastructure plan for this workload, then explain whether Bicep or Terraform is the better output format for my team.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Hub Spoke Network
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a hub-spoke Azure network with private endpoints, firewall routing, DNS, monitoring, and disaster recovery considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Approved IaC
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the approved infrastructure plan in .azure/infrastructure-plan.json, generate Terraform files under infra and include validation steps.

output_examples.input: Plan infrastructure for a production API with private data access.
output: A resource list with compute, database, Key Vault, managed identity, networking, and monitoring components.
A dependency order that explains why each resource is needed.
Security and resilience tradeoffs tied to WAF pillars.

output_examples.input: Prepare Bicep for an approved multi-resource plan.
output: A proposed infra directory layout with main template, parameter files, and service modules.
Validation steps for Bicep syntax and deployment scope.
A reminder to confirm the Azure subscription, resource group, and deployment approval.

output_examples.input: Review whether the proposed plan is production ready.
output: A checklist covering identity, secrets, monitoring, network exposure, encryption, resilience, and tagging.
A list of accepted tradeoffs and gaps that need owner approval.
Follow-up questions about traffic, data residency, budget, and operational ownership.

best_practices: Keep the infrastructure plan in draft until resource choices, dependencies, and tradeoffs are reviewed.
Validate current Azure service limits, pricing, and regional availability before deployment.
Use managed identity, Key Vault, private networking, diagnostics, and tags unless a documented tradeoff applies.
anti_patterns: Do not run deployment commands without explicit user approval and confirmed Azure context.
Do not generate IaC before the infrastructure plan is complete and approved.
Do not rely only on training data when current Azure documentation or schemas are available.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy Azure resources automatically?
faq.answer: It includes deployment guidance, but deployment should only happen after explicit approval and context verification.

faq.question: Can it generate both Bicep and Terraform?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow supports either Bicep or Terraform after the infrastructure plan is approved.

faq.question: Does it use Azure Well-Architected Framework guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow requires WAF research and checklist review before plan generation.

faq.question: Is it suitable for app-only deployments?
faq.answer: It is better for enterprise infrastructure planning. App-focused Azure workflows may need a different skill.

faq.question: What files does it create?
faq.answer: It plans to create an infrastructure plan under .azure and IaC files under infra in the project.

faq.question: Why is this audit blocked?
faq.answer: The skill contains authority language that can act like prompt injection and should be removed before publishing.
